original_abstract: This article explores the cultures of public militancy that defined the revolutionary left in France, Italy, and West Germany during the "red decade." It highlights how radical movements across these borders initially shared convergent strategic outlooks, discourses on violence, and insubordinate tactics for confronting state authorities. However, the study argues that these patterns of militancy eventually diverged along national lines, shaped by distinct legacies of civil war, varying levels of neo-fascist violence, and unique domestic social conflicts. Notably, the frequent use of lethal force by Italian police in maintaining public order prompted segments of the Italian revolutionary left to normalize the use of firearms during street protests. Ultimately, this comparative analysis demonstrates how domestic policing strategies directly conditioned the escalations of violence by activists and dictated the thin, often permeable boundary between mass protest and organized terrorism.
humanized_abstract: Abstract This article explores the cultures of public militancy of the revolutionary left in France, Italy and West Germany during the ‘red decade’ of the 1970s. The article begins by examining the initial convergence of strategic and ideological aspects of these militant cultures across borders and examining their common vocabulary of violence and disobedience in relation to the state. The article then argues for the eventual divergence of militant cultures along national lines due to differing historical experiences of civil war, neo-fascist terrorisms and domestic social conflict. Focusing on the Italian case, it is argued that the revolutionary left of Italy was conditioned to shoot by the regularity of fatal police actions to quell demonstrations and this heightened the level of lethal potential on the streets of Italian cities, far more than was the case in France or West Germany. It will be argued here that the tactics and level of activism adopted by radical movements across borders during the ‘red decade’ were conditioned by domestic policing practices and the very thin line that had to be traversed for activism to cross from the level of mass protest into that of the world of terrorism.
